My recommendation is to look for another place that will manage their venue in a professional manner or if you have the ability to spend a little more than do so. Our DJ kind of sucked my husband and I spent weeks compiling a playlist that would fit our tastes and the varied musical tastes of our guests without sounding like all the same songs you hear at every other wedding. The DJ totally ignored the playlist and actually played almost everything on our do not playlist. Okay. The floor screwed up our flowers. She used daisies all over the place. I hate daisies and the flowers were dead before the ceremony even began. My hairstylist utterly failed the day of our wedding. She was fine at our practice session, but the day actually couldn't do anything. Another girl at the salon stepped in at the last minute. I ended up leaving the appointment an hour later than planned. I didn't get to the church until less than an hour before the ceremony and I still wasn't wearing any makeup or anything. This is an important part you guys don't know yet, but this is an important part right here. The venue used wrong, the wrong wines. Due to the language and culture barrier between one bride's maid and the rest of the wedding, there were a few miscommunications with her. The biggest one was that she and the other B and bridesmaids who was supposed to be driving her ended up 20 minutes late to our outdoor pictures. That one was completely my fault for not explaining to her in clear detail what was going on. This is definitely a planner would fix. The DJ screwed up a few things like saying that my dad was the host of the event, messing up the order of the bridal party when we came into the reception, etc. Okay, I can count a number of bad dreams from which I've awoken terrified that the wedding we've been planning turned out to be a complete disaster. I have yet to have a happy one about our impending notches. This worries me to give you a sense of what these dreams are about. Here's a brief list that my in real life very sweet future mother-in-law demands, I wear a different wedding dress and I comply because I don't want her to be mad at me, that I forget to get my wedding dress tailored and thus can't wear it because it pulls in yards of extra fabric at my feet, that my fiance and I spend our entire wedding looking for each other unable to reunite, that the wedding happened but I can't remember any of it. This is the thing, this is not something I never expected. All these brides had these same terrifying dreams and the interesting thing about these terrifying dreams is the things that go wrong that ruin a wedding are one thing, usually the things they dream about are a totally different set of things but they what makes them feel terrified. And it's just, I never, I saw this on several sites and stuff, ask anybody if she's had bad dreams leading up to her big day and we bet she says yes, turns out wedding nightmares aren't all that uncommon thanks to the huge amount of stress that goes into planning a wedding. The nightmare, my wedding nightmare which I've had a few times is that there's suddenly a problem with my dress and I have to find a new one. My recurring wedding nightmare was that my hairstylist wouldn't show up for the morning of my wedding, I couldn't get into my dress. Two things that they seem to be worried about the most is the groom is MIA and they look terrible. Get some other ones. There, no one is ready, forget the wedding, this is they wake up and they forget it's their wedding day, how could they ever forget it's their wedding day? And by the way, I played volleyball with this girl who's getting married in two months and she makes excuse for everything she does because she's getting married in two months. If she can't play ball accurately, she can't serve correctly. Everything is blamed that she's getting married in two months. I think it's actually a couple of weeks but so it's every other sentence out of her is blame my wedding. Okay, one night dream that I never sent the limitations out and only a few people showed. The photographer and DJ were MIA and the efficient said she had to leave to do another wedding because we were supposed to have her ceremony at 530 and for some reason it was after 6. I wasn't wearing my dress either and we wound up having a ceremony in the middle of a dining hall with like five family members. I jumped at my venue bright down and had to move my wedding to a hotel that was hosting a children's pageant. When I went to get ready for the ceremony I found that my dress had been somehow switched with a child's pageant dress. I also had the moment of realization that I was completely ungroomed. Then I realized I had no idea where my bridesmaid were. I was just amazed at how I didn't know how much they went through. I dreamed I dreamt I haven't showered and I haven't had my makeup done had put on my dress backwards. I have these dreams. It's ranged from my dad only giving me some minuscule amount of money to pay for it to no one showing up and having everything I wanted there not be there. The second one is just so angry and doucey. It was exact, you know, it's like, and also one of the things I learned was, is that her wedding day is the day after or the minute or the second after he proposes, is her wedding day. It's everything that she's doing up until that day. That's her whole experience. And when you're hiring a wedding planner and a coordinator, that whole experience is part of that process. And so, getting the right coordinator all along is part of that experience and part of what you're taking care of. So anyhow, so that's what I got into. I wanted to talk about voice and just, I think I got like one last frame, and that was to point this out about the value of voice. And I don't know if anybody here is old enough to remember Phil Donahue and Oprah Winfrey. Well, Phil Donahue used to be this talk show guy, and he was a guy that was like, you know, probably establishment, I think he might have been a psychiatrist or a therapist or something, but he held this show like a talk show, like it was a debate, and he would run through it, and he was just trying to play devil's advocate on both sides of an issue. And I think the audience, which was mostly female, was there because they didn't have any other options. And here is the guy trying to intellectualize some stuff. But when Oprah came along, Schelper was going to blow him out of the water, but even if she wasn't Oprah, the fact was that Oprah connected with people, she connected with people related to them, she empathized with them, she felt where Donahue was trying to be establishment kind of thing. So I mean, here's an older white guy with a young black woman, and she just like, just took over, right? And I think that you could maybe say apples and oranges, maybe his talk show was about this, maybe hers was about that. But I swear, even if he took Oprah and put her in Phil Donahue's body, but Phil Donahue and Oprah's body, Oprah's still going to win because Oprah still had her voice, and her voice was always going to resonate with her target audience. Donahue could resonate with different people, but Oprah was resonating with that audience that was in that time slot that was watching those shows, and the dominant viewing public. And that's what I'm trying to do here, this is the way to dig in, to get the, to listen in and get that message and hear the voice of the people and know where they're coming from, and what's important to them. And you know, because there's a lot of ways to look at saying go, well, wedding's important, but you don't know how it's important and where it's important. And so, yeah, so that's my lesson, and I hope you guys can see how I'm plucking out things and getting to them, and how if you're not, everyone on this webinar is not looking to become a wedding planner, but you can see how you can go into, it's the Amazon reviews, into the Yelp reviews, into some of the sites and some of the blogs and stuff, and see how and where you can see, if you can see how I highlighted certain things. First of all, one of the things, when you do with the voice, is you want to understand whether or not you're trying to be the exact voice of your prospect or the voice of authority the prospect wants to hear. And one of the tricks that Kevin kind of alluded to when you're the voice of authority, is you drop those terms that they knew B might not know. And I think it was day of commencement, not day of coordinator. But because the first one said day of commencement, the second one said day of coordinate. I would do more homework, I was actually ready for it, but yeah. Well, but what you want to do is if you're being authority, what you want to do is you drop that term and then you spell it out. But you also spell it out and what you're looking for is a term that's so technical that you need to spell it out in plain English. Because what you're doing is using the term proves your authority and spelling it out in plain English makes people feel safe that they're never going to get lost in your presence. That you're not going to leave them wanting. But you'll do that less if you're trying to write it from the first person like a referral type of promotion. So if it was coming from the housewife in Ohio who's doing this and doing that, like the code of arms letter, you might not want to drop a technical research term or whatever because you're trying to be the voice of the prospect. But if you're being the voice of the authority, you look for those terms specifically so that you can do that trick in dropping them. So for example, the one thing that I got out of this that I would really go key on is explain, listen, you know, your wedding really started right after he asked you to marry and you started planning and your anxiety is going to start building. The great thing about our wedding coordinator is you're going to sleep well at night and enjoy the process of the wedding. She's got the flexibility to change the things on the fly and wait till you hear, you know, see the professionalism that she puts in. You know, she doesn't work with anybody and she has no vendors that she hasn't worked with with over 12 weddings to prove that they're reliable. She's got backup plans for all of it, her checklist, and then you list all those things she carries with her spare nail polish kits in case your nails are chipped, invisible ink thread, extra large umbrellas, and that's the other key. Marketing, I always say, is about hooks, offers, and solutions. And in every one of these things that you see a problem, if you think of a solution, this proves why doing the research is better than creating the product. Because if I was a wedding coordinator and doing this research, I'd be buying those big golf umbrellas, you know, so that this is in the car and this is in my toolkit and I'd be advertising what's in my toolkit kind of, you know, or showing that this is the kind of level of preparedness, you know, that I've seen all these disasters and you know, so she carries an extra wide golf umbrella to make sure that you don't, your hair doesn't get messed up on the rainy day. And you think of the solutions and another solution I thought of, which because you're talking about is say, you know, and unlike so many other horror stories, you'll never have to sue this wedding coordinator because she's got built in financial penalties and money she's got to give back if the DJ doesn't play the right songs and if the flowers are dead, or if you know, these things are happening and you probably won't have to do that. But the point is, you know, you're putting the mind at ease and say, you know, now you can enjoy the process, start sleeping well from right now up until your magical day. And the only thing you're going to remember is the joy of planning it, the joy of experiencing it, the commencement, and how smoothly it went. You know, this wedding planner is also going to be concerned to make sure that you're the primary person to be made happy, but she's going to work to also make everybody happy. You know, if she got a pocket full of value in case your groom gets jitters, she's got a list of preset speeches in case your best man gets nervous, you know, that they can read, you know. And so the whole thing that you're going through this, I'm thinking of, oh, this would be a great way if I was a wedding planner. The last thing I'd like to say is, you know, when you go into some of these places for research, sometimes it's a form, like I did an experiment like this that you're talking about that you did, you did a great job, Kevin. I did it with musicians. And the reason I did it with musicians is I don't know anything about music and guitars and everything. And I learned all these things about, you know, the local gigging bands and, you know, the phrases that they wanted. In fact, I even forgot the phrases I had used. You know, that I reiterated and I ran it by a musician. He's like, yeah, it sounds like you know what you're talking about, right? I don't. I have no idea. But it was able to speak in the prospect, but it was an interactive form that I went to. And the beauty of that is you can ask questions. And so what you want to do is if you want to anonymously, you don't go in there and say, I'm marketing this or I want to build up this and you're, you know, you're going to get killed on Reddit or whatever if you do that. But what you could do is, you know, you act like a member of the forum and you say, you know, hey, you know, when it comes to, when it comes to a custom made wedding dress, what, you know, what's your, what's your dream dress? And then they'll start using emotional adjectives and adverbs that you want to pay attention to. And then you say, hey, what's your biggest nightmare when it comes to, you know, you just spent $5,000 on a watch, you know, what's, you know, what's your biggest nightmare in whatever the product or service is? And if, so if you're in those places where you get to interact with people and you can ask them those kinds of questions, they'll come back with emotional adverbs and adjectives to that you can add to the voice to make sure that you're kind of resonating with it. So you kind of already covered that. One thing I would say is I would test those hooks. How, you know, you know, I mean, these are all the different kind of angles I would hit, but the body copy probably wouldn't change much at all. Kevin and I would write, we would attack every single issue that was a common thing and talk about it in the positive way that you won't have to deal with this and you won't have to deal with that. But we would test each of the different hooks to see which got the most attention and, you know, spawn the most and then do it. But yes, that we did. The one thing I would think of is if I was in competition with Amazon, I think I would want to go negative, which I, I don't think brides always want negative. You know, they want fairytale, everything's going to go crazy. Butterflies and rainbows and unicorns, not a problem, but that's all over Amazon. Do you know what I'm saying? That all over Amazon is perfect wedding, you know, I think I agree there. I would go into this free book is going to tell you how to, how to sleep at night from these nightmares you're having. And then, you know, that would get them to read, it would be free. And then it would point to disasters that were avoidable. If you'd hired a wedding planner or a day of coordinator, a day of ceremony person. And then it, but all of it would be like Bond said, it would be on a positive, it would be on a positive stand. The first part I would open up with as a strategy is I would say, I used to have these nightmares and they were this, this, this and this. And not even admit that everyone else has it, just say I have it. So they just go, oh my God, she's just like me. I'm not the only one. Blah, blah, blah. That's when I started my life as a wedding coordinator, because I knew what it was like to go through this thing alone and feel alone and know that my bridesmaids were just as inexperienced as I was and had never faced these problems before. And I needed someone that could be the bad guy when things went wrong and handle things in the background so I could focus on my wedding. And these are the things that I would make sure didn't happen. And I saw nightmare avoided, nightmare avoided, nightmare avoided, you know what I'm saying? Even as, as I started to hear some of the stories brides had gone through, it scared the hell out of me. For example, and that, you know, then you can go into these nightmares because I think every bride goes into this thing in this fairy tale wonderful thing. And until you remind them that stuff can go wrong and it can really ruin the biggest day of your life. I mean, I think that's very important. I think the nightmare is a big part of it. But I, and I don't think they're really thinking about the nightmare until you do that. So, yeah, I mean, would you say that you kind of got to say, you know, I'd heard these horror stories and then go into the, but I would still, I would still out that I was a colorful person of positivity that was going to make it magical. Yeah, but you can't leave that part out. Because if you just, then you're buying two coordinators, one to make your life happy, the other to keep it from falling apart. I can tell you guys for all of you who are in hybridology, I'm going to admit something. I've had two weddings. The first one was the most awful hideous thing. I actually once gave a humor speech about it because my premise of the speech was God was screaming at me, do not marry this man. And I ignored him. And you see how that went. The second one, though, I did everything myself. I didn't have a coordinator, but I kind of was my own coordinator. But I, you know, you guys don't know me too well, but when the bad guy needs to come out, I can be the bad guy. I can be the bad guy. You don't want to be the bad guy, right? You don't want to be the bad guy. No, when the florist, the florist was fantastic, but we rented all these firms and there weren't enough. And I made them go back and get more. That day, though, my second wedding, I was such a nervous wreck because I had been in charge of everything. I was sitting in the kitchen of the country club with my head in my hands, trying not to throw up on my wedding gown because that's how stressed out I was. And these women, if you can inhabit, it doesn't matter. Michael, you said you spent too much time on message boards and you know all of these, it's a slingo. If you can put yourself in the mindset of that bride who can't keep anything more than a couple of sips of Coca-Cola down, because she's terrified, she's going to puke all over her dress, because she's not only, she's nervous about the wedding and everything that could go wrong, but she's tying herself obsessively for the rest of her life to another person. And if you can get inside that mindset and channel that, and it's the same thing for any product. Michael Forten yesterday was talking about his 70-plus-year-old golfers. You inhabit that mindset. And I know David Brits for Financial Products and he's got to inhabit the mind of the trader who wants to just be able to sit around and look at his charts and make money all day. We get to be kind of, Mark has said this before, so I'll point this out since he's not here, it's his absentia moment. Being a copywriter in that kind of a level is kind of like being an actor, because you get to imagine and try on all of these different personas and be all of these different people. And the difference is an actor gets to have their performance edited by someone else. As a copywriter, you get to perfect all of it before anybody else sees it. So it's really kind of the best of both worlds. You get to play pretend and you get to make it perfect while you're at it. So that was just my two cents. The nice thing is if you could test this out, because with Amazon, Bond knows all this stuff, you can try all this stuff and then change the book and update it so much that you've changed the whole angle and see you can almost flip test the damn thing. You can change the descriptions and all that. You find testing where you can. I was explaining to somebody else, if I'm going to test hooks, I'll test them on my Facebook page and then take something, if it's garnering a lot more likes and a lot more love than I know is something that's better to roll out with. But yeah, I mean, the answer to everything in direct marketing is test. And let's not forget one last thing, which is it's only if possible. I've done the one on the guitar and it would take me forever to do it. And Kevin is not going to go get married to do the research. But having going through the process is also invaluable if possible. You do need to do that if that's an opportunity. If somebody sent Kevin, they're like, I want you to learn some kite surfing. I think it was kite surfing stuff. If Kevin's able and he's willing, he'll go down and try kite surfing. So now he can talk about the frustrations of learning, the learning curve, what made things faster. And he will have even more experience with that. I've been married. I'm the only one who hasn't.
